ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Pig's Stomach Soup; closest defensible match is pork stomach soup food product under FOODON organ-meat soup hierarchy.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pig stomach — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for pork organ-meat soup preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Pig's Stomach Soup as a prepared dish; A0FJP (pig stomach) is the closest defensible ingredient-level anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Pig's Stomach Soup is approximately one bowl of 300 g, comprising a portion of sliced pig stomach in peppery broth with ginkgo nuts and aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Pig Stomach, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition of Foods for pork organ soup context
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Pig's Stomach Soup as a prepared dish exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pork stomach (FDC ID 168375) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore pork organ soup data. Broth dilution factor applied to reflect soup composition at approximately 40–45% solid content. Input JSON values (enriched_json, confidence 0.90–0.95) are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained with minor adjustments for saturated fat and cholesterol fields not present in the input.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate for high-protein, low-carbohydrate pork organ soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pig's Stomach Soup. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate pork broth soups from the University of Sydney GI Database. The soup contains only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from ginkgo nuts and trace aromatics; the high protein and collagen content further attenuates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 7.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.6, adjusted to 2.1 after dietary fibre subtraction.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (180 mg per 100 g; ~540 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one bowl and avoid adding extra salt or soy sauce, as a single 300 g serving contributes approximately 540 mg sodium, roughly 23% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g) and high protein (12.5 g per 100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pig's Stomach Soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content and estimated low glycaemic index of 35; the high protein and collagen content slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial blood glucose rise, making it a clinically appropriate protein-rich soup choice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol (~55 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (~1.3 g per 100 g) from pork organ meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Pig's Stomach Soup in moderation; the relatively low saturated fat content is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet, but sodium intake should be monitored and the soup should be prepared without excessive added salt or fatty pork additions to keep saturated fat and sodium within recommended limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Collagen-rich pig stomach and anti-inflammatory white peppercorn compounds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The collagen content of pig stomach may support joint tissue health, and white peppercorns contain piperine which has demonstrated mild anti-inflammatory properties in preclinical studies; however, individuals on low-purine diets should moderate organ-meat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pig's Stomach Soup (猪肚汤 / 豬肚湯, Zhū Dǔ Tāng) is a traditional Chinese restorative soup deeply rooted in Cantonese and Teochew culinary traditions, widely consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, and southern China. The dish is prepared by meticulously cleaning a whole pig stomach — scrubbing with salt and flour to remove odour — then slow-simmering it for two to three hours until the stomach is tender yet retains a characteristic slightly chewy, gelatinous texture. The defining flavour profile is intensely peppery and warming, achieved by adding a generous quantity of crushed or whole white peppercorns to the broth, which are simmered long enough to release their volatile oils and produce a throat-warming heat. Ginkgo nuts are a classic addition, contributing a subtly bitter-sweet flavour and firm texture. Aromatics such as ginger, garlic, and sometimes dried tofu skin or pork ribs are added to enrich the broth. Nutritionally, the soup is high in protein (approximately 12.5 g per 100 g) and relatively low in fat (3.8 g per 100 g) and carbohydrates (2.5 g per 100 g), yielding approximately 95 kcal per 100 g. It is a meaningful source of vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg per 100 g), niacin (3.5 mg), zinc (1.8 mg), and selenium (12.5 mcg). The collagen-rich pig stomach contributes to the soup's traditional reputation in Chinese medicine as a digestive tonic and warming food. Clinically, the soup is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycaemic index (~35) and minimal carbohydrate load; sodium content (180 mg per 100 g) warrants monitoring for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The soup is not halal due to the use of pork.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Pig's Stomach Soup as a prepared dish exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore nutrient databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork stomach data (FDC ID 168375) with broth dilution adjustment, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore pork organ soup context. Input JSON values (enriched_json, confidence 0.90–0.95) are consistent with this adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked pork stomach, FDC ID 168375) with a broth dilution factor to reflect soup composition; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore pork organ soup data. No exact prepared-dish entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. saturated_fat_g (1.3 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.5 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6 g), cholesterol_mg (55.0 mg) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork stomach data adjusted for soup dilution; these fields were absent from the input JSON. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this prepared soup is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database entries consulted. 4. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace for minimally processed pork organ soup per USDA SR Legacy data. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate pork broth soups from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Pig's Stomach Soup was found. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (35 × 7.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 2.6, adjusted to 2.1 after dietary fibre subtraction. 6. The dish is not halal (contains pork). 7. Portions in the input JSON contained a duplicate entry (two identical 300 g servings); deduplicated to a single 300 g default serving. 8. Input source 'Journal of Food and Nutrition Research / sciepub.com' is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore as the primary nutrient evidence basis. 9. Q8 fix (iteration 3): all strings present in aliases_by_language — including 'Zhū Dǔ Tāng' and 'Zhu Du Tang' in the romanized array — have been verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Self-check passed.
